subsystem: HHVM (HipHop Virtual Machine) WDDX serializer. WddxPacket::recursiveAddVar walks a PHP value (scalar, array, or object) and appends its WDDX/XML wire representation to the packet's accumulating output string (m_packetString). WDDX is an XML-based data-exchange serialization format; this routine is the recursive encoder that turns runtime PHP variables into the <struct>/<array>/<var>/<string> element tree.
provenance: Identified by the type vocabulary and API surface, all HHVM runtime types: HHVM's String / Variant / Array / Object / ArrayIter classes, the WddxPacket class and its m_packetString member, o_getClassName() (HHVM object-class accessor), StringUtil::HtmlEncode with StringUtil::QuoteStyle::Double, and helpers getDataTypeString()/getWddxEncoded(). The file banner is corpus/train/train_0279_cwe79.cpp and the in-code marker '/* sample: primevul_train_2079_fixed */' ties it to the PrimeVul corpus; the WDDX/<struct>/<var name=...> XML vocabulary confirms it is the ext_wddx serializer in the HHVM runtime.
trust_boundary: The PHP value graph passed as varVariant is application data that is frequently attacker-influenced: WDDX serialization is applied to request/session/user data, and array KEYS (varName) and object CLASS NAMES flow straight from PHP-land where an attacker can control array keys and, via crafted objects, class names. The output m_packetString is XML that downstream consumers parse and that may be reflected into HTML/XML sinks (the file is tagged cwe79 = XSS/output-neutralization). Hostile fields are therefore: varName (attribute text), the object class name emitted at o_getClassName(), and the scalar value; everything that is concatenated into the XML without neutralization is a candidate injection point.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The recursive WDDX encoder. varName is the name/key under which this value is being emitted (e.g. an array key or the top-level variable name), passed by const reference as an HHVM String. varVariant is the PHP value being serialized (an HHVM Variant, which can hold scalars, arrays, or objects). hasVarTag controls whether this invocation wraps its output in a <var name='...'> element (true for named members, false for bare array elements). The function returns bool to signal whether it emitted anything. Because it calls itself for nested containers, varName/varVariant at each level come from container iteration, i.e. from data, not from a fixed schema.

The value is classified: isArray and isObject probe the Variant's runtime type. The whole first block (lines 9-65) handles container types (arrays and objects); the scalar path below (lines 67-81) handles everything else. This split decides which encoding branch runs and, importantly, determines whether recursion continues (containers recurse; scalars terminate).

When this container is a named member (hasVarTag true), the code opens a WDDX <var> element and writes the member name into the single-quoted name attribute. varName.data() returns the raw character buffer of the HHVM String and is concatenated directly into the XML with NO escaping — the name is placed verbatim between the opening `<var name='` and the closing `'>`. This is the attacker-reachable attribute sink: whatever bytes are in varName become part of the XML attribute text unmodified.

The container is normalized into an Array view (varAsArray) that can be iterated regardless of whether it started as a PHP array or object. varAsObject holds the object form (used later to read the class name). For arrays, varAsArray is taken directly; for objects, the object is converted to an array of its properties. From here the code iterates varAsArray uniformly.

length is the element count of the normalized array (a signed int). If there are elements (length > 0) the non-empty branch runs; otherwise the empty branch at line 49 runs. A heuristic then promotes the value to 'object' encoding if the FIRST key is a string (line 24): a PHP array whose first key is a string is serialized as a WDDX <struct> rather than an <array>. This is a shape-detection convenience so associative arrays render as structs.

The opening container tag is written. For struct/object encoding it emits <struct>, and — only when the original value was a genuine object (!isArray) — it additionally emits a php_class_name member whose <string> body is the object's class name via varAsObject->o_getClassName().c_str(), again concatenated with NO escaping. For plain arrays it emits <array length='N'> using std::to_string(length), which is a safe numeric conversion. The class-name insertion is a second raw, attacker-influenced sink (object class names can be crafted).

The core recursion: every element of the container is walked. For each pair, key becomes the varName of the child call (via key.toString()) and value becomes the child varVariant. hasVarTag for the child is isObject — struct members are wrapped in <var name='...'>, array elements are not. Because recursiveAddVar calls itself here, the entire nested value graph is serialized depth-first, and the recursion depth equals the nesting depth of the input data.
After iterating, the matching closing tag is emitted: </struct> for object/struct encoding, </array> for array encoding. This closes the non-empty container branch opened at line 22.

The empty-container branch (length <= 0). An empty object still emits an empty <struct>...</struct>, again including the raw php_class_name/o_getClassName().c_str() when it is a real object. An empty plain array emits nothing here. Note this duplicates the same unescaped class-name emission as lines 28-30, so the raw class-name sink exists on both the populated and empty code paths.
If the container was wrapped in a <var> element at line 10, the matching </var> is closed here, and the container branch returns true (something was emitted). This ends the array/object handling.

The scalar path. varType is the WDDX type string for this value (e.g. 'boolean', 'string', 'number'). The guard calls getWddxEncoded with an empty value to test whether this type is serializable at all; if it is, the value is materialized: booleans become the literals 'true'/'false', and everything else is run through StringUtil::HtmlEncode with QuoteStyle::Double before being stored in varValue. This HtmlEncode call is the neutralization that makes the scalar VALUE safe against XML/HTML injection — it is the mitigation this 'fixed' sample applies. The encoded value plus the (still-raw) varName are then handed to getWddxEncoded, whose output is appended to the packet. Note varName is passed here unescaped, so its neutralization depends on getWddxEncoded's implementation (not shown).
If the value was neither a container nor a serializable scalar (getWddxEncoded returned empty), the function emits nothing and returns false, signalling that this variable produced no WDDX output.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: varName is written into the single-quoted XML attribute with zero neutralization (varName.data() is the raw string buffer). varName originates from array keys via key.toString() at line 40, which an attacker can control. A key containing a single quote closes the attribute early, and characters like `<`, `>`, or `'>` let the attacker inject arbitrary attributes or sibling XML/HTML elements into the WDDX document (e.g. a key of `x'><script>...` breaks out of the attribute). Because the value branch at lines 73-75 is explicitly HtmlEncode'd but this name branch is not, the name is the residual injection sink (CWE-79 / improper neutralization / XML injection). The invariant: any attacker-influenced text placed into the WDDX attribute or element body must be XML/HTML-entity-encoded (at minimum the single quote and angle brackets) before concatenation, exactly as the scalar value is.

The object's class name is inserted verbatim into the <string> body with no escaping. PHP class names are normally constrained, but anonymous classes, closures, and dynamically named/generated classes can carry attacker-influenced characters; any `<`, `>`, or `&` in the class name is emitted raw into the WDDX XML, again bypassing the HtmlEncode that protects scalar values. This is the same CWE-79 output-neutralization defect as the name attribute, on a different sink. The identical raw emission is duplicated in the empty-object branch at lines 54-56, so the fix (encode before append) must be applied to both sites. Invariant: the class-name string must be entity-encoded before being placed inside the <string> element.

recursiveAddVar recurses once per nesting level with no depth limit or cycle guard. A deeply nested PHP array/object graph (e.g. an array nested thousands of levels deep, trivially built by an attacker who controls the value being serialized) drives the native C++ call stack until it overflows, crashing the request handler (denial of service). Self-referential structures are dampened by PHP's copy-on-read/array semantics, but pure depth is enough. This is uncontrolled recursion (CWE-674). Invariant: the serializer must bound recursion depth (a max-depth counter that aborts with an error) rather than trusting input nesting depth.

On the scalar path the value (varValue) IS HtmlEncode'd, but varName is forwarded to getWddxEncoded raw. If getWddxEncoded embeds varName into a <var name='...'> attribute the same way the container branch does at line 12, the scalar path inherits the identical unescaped-name injection. This is a latent contract on the not-shown getWddxEncoded implementation: it must neutralize varName, or callers must pre-encode it. Worth confirming because the encoding of the name is inconsistent with the deliberate encoding of the value.

Lifetime note (dismissed as safe, recorded for completeness): o_getClassName() returns a temporary HHVM String and .c_str() yields a pointer into that temporary's buffer. Because the pointer is consumed immediately by operator+= within the same full-expression, the temporary outlives the copy and there is no use-after-free here — unlike the classic (a+b).c_str() dangling-pointer pattern where the pointer is stored or returned. The same reasoning applies to varName.data() at line 12. No finding, but any refactor that stores these pointers across statements would introduce CWE-416.

Minor robustness note (dismissed): length is a signed int taken from Array::length(). Array sizes in HHVM are bounded well below INT_MAX in practice, so the > 0 guard and std::to_string(length) at line 34 are not realistic overflow surfaces here; the value is used only for a numeric attribute, not for buffer indexing. The first-key-is-string heuristic at line 24 can misclassify an array as a struct, which is a fidelity quirk, not a memory-safety issue.

summary: A WDDX/XML serializer that correctly HtmlEncodes scalar VALUES (the applied fix) but still concatenates the member NAME (line 12) and the object CLASS NAME (lines 29 and 55) into the XML with no neutralization, leaving attacker-controllable array keys and class names as XML/HTML injection sinks (CWE-79). Secondary risk is unbounded recursion (line 40) over attacker-nested data, a stack-exhaustion DoS (CWE-674).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: recursiveAddVar(const String& varName, const Variant& varVariant, bool hasVarTag) — signature, lines 2-4
reviewed_anchors.reason: varName is attacker-controllable (array key) and reaches an unescaped XML attribute sink; the recursive self-call has no depth bound.
Raw, unescaped insertion of an attacker-influenced name into a single-quoted XML attribute (CWE-79).
Raw, unescaped class name inserted into the <string> body (CWE-79).
Duplicate unescaped class-name sink on the empty-container branch; same CWE-79 defect, must be fixed at both sites.
Unbounded recursion driven by input nesting depth (CWE-674 stack exhaustion / DoS).
varName forwarded unescaped; latent injection depends on the not-shown getWddxEncoded neutralizing the name.
This IS the neutralization/mitigation for the scalar value; correct behavior, not a defect (it is the reference safe pattern the name sinks lack).
Pointers are consumed within the same full-expression by operator+=, so the temporary outlives the copy; no use-after-free (CWE-416).
Used only as a numeric attribute via std::to_string; not an index or buffer size, and HHVM array sizes are bounded well below INT_MAX.
Shape-detection heuristic; can misclassify array vs struct (fidelity quirk) but carries no memory-safety or injection contract.
Numeric-to-string conversion of a bounded integer; not attacker-controllable text, safe to concatenate.
Type-name string drawn from a fixed internal vocabulary, not attacker-controlled content.

findings.title: Member name written into WDDX XML attribute without neutralization (XML/HTML injection)
findings.bug_class: Improper neutralization of special elements in output (XSS / XML attribute injection)
findings.invariant: Any attacker-influenced string concatenated into the WDDX XML — attribute values and element bodies alike — must be XML/HTML-entity-encoded (single quote, angle brackets, ampersand) before being appended, the same way the scalar value is HtmlEncode'd at lines 73-75.
findings.failure_mode: varName comes from array keys (key.toString() at line 40), which an attacker controls. A key containing a single quote closes the name attribute; characters like `'><...>` let the attacker inject arbitrary attributes or new XML/HTML elements into the serialized packet. Downstream consumers that render or re-parse the WDDX document then process attacker-injected markup, enabling XSS or structural XML injection. This defect is highlighted by contrast: the value branch is deliberately encoded while this name branch is not.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no encoding is applied to varName before line 12 and that key.toString() can carry quote/angle-bracket bytes; verify whether any downstream sink renders the packet as HTML/XML.

findings.title: Object class name emitted into WDDX <string> body without neutralization
findings.bug_class: Improper neutralization of special elements in output (XSS / XML injection)
findings.invariant: The object class-name string must be XML/HTML-entity-encoded before being placed inside the <string> element, at BOTH emission sites (the populated branch here and the empty-object branch at lines 54-56).
findings.failure_mode: o_getClassName() is inserted raw into the XML body. Anonymous classes, closures, and dynamically generated class names can carry characters such as `<`, `>`, or `&`; these are emitted verbatim, allowing markup injection into the WDDX document that bypasses the value-only HtmlEncode. Impact mirrors the name-attribute injection (XSS / structural XML corruption). The identical unescaped emission is duplicated on the empty-object path (line 55), so a partial fix would leave one sink open.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm o_getClassName() output is not pre-sanitized and that both the populated (line 29) and empty (line 55) branches lack encoding; check whether class names can be attacker-influenced (anonymous/dynamic classes).

findings.title: Unbounded recursion over attacker-nested containers (stack exhaustion)
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled recursion / stack exhaustion (denial of service)
findings.invariant: Serialization recursion depth must be bounded by an explicit max-depth counter that aborts encoding when exceeded, rather than being driven purely by the nesting depth of the input value graph.
findings.failure_mode: The function recurses once per container nesting level with no depth cap. An attacker who controls the value being WDDX-serialized can supply a structure nested thousands of levels deep, driving the native C++ call stack to overflow and crashing the request/worker (DoS). No cycle or depth guard is present in the shown code.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no enclosing depth limit around WDDX serialization and measure the stack cost per recursion level against the thread stack size.

findings.title: Latent: varName forwarded unescaped to getWddxEncoded on the scalar path
findings.bug_class: Improper neutralization of special elements in output (latent, in callee)
findings.invariant: getWddxEncoded must XML-encode the varName it embeds (or callers must pre-encode it); the value is HtmlEncode'd here, but the name is not.
findings.failure_mode: The scalar branch carefully HtmlEncodes varValue (lines 73-75) but passes varName raw into getWddxEncoded. If getWddxEncoded places varName into a <var name='...'> attribute the way the container branch does at line 12, the scalar path inherits the same attacker-controlled-name injection. The bug lives in the not-shown getWddxEncoded contract.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect getWddxEncoded to confirm whether it entity-encodes the varName argument before embedding it in the emitted <var name='...'> element.

audit_checklist: Apply XML/HTML entity-encoding to varName before line 12 (encode single quote and angle brackets), matching the value-side HtmlEncode.
Encode o_getClassName() output before appending at BOTH line 29 and line 55; do not leave the empty-object branch unpatched.
Add an explicit recursion depth limit around recursiveAddVar (line 40) that aborts on excessive nesting to prevent stack-exhaustion DoS.
Verify getWddxEncoded neutralizes the varName argument it embeds (line 77), so the scalar path is not a residual name-injection sink.
Confirm array keys (key.toString(), line 40) and object class names can carry quote/angle-bracket bytes, i.e. that these sinks are genuinely attacker-reachable.
Confirm .c_str()/.data() results (lines 12, 29, 55) are only ever consumed within the same statement so no dangling-pointer refactor is introduced.
